name: Dummy Deck
description: Sandbox for testing new technical phrases before migration.

cards.sideA: Ambos sistemas en 31.0.5 están sincronizados.

sideB.text: Both systems on 31.0.5 are in sync.
Both systems are synchronized on 31.0.5.
Both systems running 31.0.5 are in sync.
Both systems on 31.0.5 are synchronized.
Both systems on 31.0.5 match.
Both systems on 31.0.5 are matching.

cards.note:
**[195]**

~Both systems on 31.0.5 are sincronized~ is misspelled. The correct spelling is [synchronized].

~Both systems on 31.0.5 are sync~ is grammatically incorrect. You must use the preposition [in sync] or the past participle [synced].

~Both systems in 31.0.5 are synchronized~ is incorrect because we use the preposition [on] when referring to software versions, platforms, or operating systems.

cards.sideA: Ya lo había revisado, pero deberé hacerle un doble check

sideB.text: I had already checked it, but I'll have to double-check.
I've reviewed it already, but I should double-check it.
I already reviewed it, but I will need to double-check it.
I checked it already, but I'll have to double-check.
I already checked it, but I'll have to double-check.
I already checked it, but I'll have to double-check it.
I had already checked it, but I'll have to double-check it.

cards.note:
**[7]**

[I already checked it, but I'll have to double-check it] is completely valid. Using the Past Simple with 'already' is very common and natural in conversational American English, even though the Past Perfect is technically more precise.

cards.sideA: Estamos esperando que la base de datos se sincronice.

sideB.text: We are waiting for the database to sync.
We're waiting for the database to synchronize.
We are waiting for the DB to be synced.
We're waiting for the database to get synchronized.

cards.note:
**[194]**

The verb "wait" is a classic trigger. We say [wait for] + [the database] + [to sync]. ~Waiting that the database syncs~ is an unnatural direct translation from Spanish.

~We're waiting for the database gets synchronized~ is incorrect because after the structure [wait for] + [object], you must use the infinitive form with [to], not a conjugated verb.

cards.sideA: ¿Cuál es el puerto que estás escuchando?

sideB.text: Which port are you listening on?
What port are you listening on?
On which port are you listening?

cards.note:
**[198]**

~Which is the port you are listening on?~ is structurally clunky and unnatural. The standard phrasing attaches the question word directly to the noun: [Which port are you listening on?].

It is not a strict grammar error, but it forces an unnecessary separation. The core rule for natural questions is to establish the specific subject of inquiry immediately by pairing the Question Word and the Noun: [Which port] + [Auxiliary] + [Subject] + [Verb].

~Which port are you listening to?~ is incorrect because in IT and networking, a server or application listens [on] a specific port, not "to" it.

cards.sideA: ¿Cuál es el puerto que debo escuchar?

sideB.text: Which port should I listen on?
What port should I listen on?
On which port should I listen?
Which port do I have to listen on?

cards.note:
**[198]**

~Which is the port I should listen on?~ is structurally clunky and unnatural. It is much more direct and standard to attach the question word directly to the noun: [Which port should I listen on?].

While ~Which is the port I should listen on?~ is technically grammatical (it forms a relative clause: 'the port [that] I should listen on'), it is highly inefficient for conversation. The rule for natural phrasing is to group the target noun immediately with the question word: [Which port] + [Auxiliary] + [Subject] + [Verb].

~Which one port should I listen on?~ is redundant. You must use either [Which port] or [Which one], but not both together.

cards.sideA: Estaré atento.

sideB.text: I'll keep an eye out.
I'll be on the lookout.
I'll stay tuned.
I'll pay attention.
I'll watch out for it.
I'll be ready.
I'll watch out.

cards.note:
**[199]**

~I'll be aware~ translates more to 'Estaré consciente' (having knowledge of a fact), rather than actively watching for an event. To express active anticipation, use phrases like [keep an eye out] or [watch for it].

[Keep an eye out] is a fixed idiom. You cannot substitute the verb and say ~have an eye out~.

cards.sideA: ¿Cuánto ha crecido el proyecto?

sideB.text: How much has the project grown?
To what extent has the project grown?
How much did the project grow?

cards.note:
**[5]**

~How much does the proyect grow?~ has a spelling error ('project', not 'proyect') and uses the Present Simple tense. Since the original phrase asks about a past action with relevance to the present ('ha crecido'), you must use the Present Perfect: [How much has the project grown?].

~How has the project grown?~ translates to "¿Cómo ha crecido el proyecto?" (asking about the manner or method of growth). To ask "¿Cuánto...?" (asking for a quantity or extent), you must use [How much].

cards.sideA: Por favor, conéctate al servidor 2.

sideB.text: Please connect to server 2.
Please log in to server 2.
Make sure to connect to server 2.

cards.note:
**[196]**

~Connect to the server 2~ is incorrect. Drop the article 'the' and place the noun before the number: [server 2].

cards.sideA: Sí, estaré atento al email.

sideB.text: Yes, I'll keep an eye out for the email.
Yes, I'll be on the lookout for the email.
Yes, I'll watch out for the email.

cards.note:
**[199]**

~Yes, I'll have an eye out for the email~ is incorrect. The standard idiom uses the verb [keep], as in [keep an eye out].

~Yes, I'll keep an eye for the email~ is incorrect because it is missing the particle [out]. The complete phrasal verb is [keep an eye out for].

~Yes, I'll keep an eye out on the email~ mixes two different idioms. You use [keep an eye out for] when waiting for something to arrive, and [keep an eye on] when monitoring something that is already there.

cards.sideA: Sí, estaré atento en la hoja de trabajo.

sideB.text: Yes, I'll keep an eye out on the spreadsheet.
Yes, I'll be on the lookout in the spreadsheet.
Yes, I'll watch for it in the spreadsheet.
Yes, I'll keep an eye out for the spreadsheet.

cards.note:
**[199]**

A [spreadsheet] is an electronic document arranged in a grid of rows and columns, like Excel or Google Sheets.

cards.sideA: La dirección de la página comienza en 894.

sideB.text: The page address starts with 894.
The URL begins with 894.
The address of the page starts with 894.

cards.note: ~The page direction starts with 894~ is incorrect because [direction] refers to physical orientation or instructions. A digital or physical location must be translated as [address] or [URL].

cards.sideA: El ciclo HTTP contiene una petición y una respuesta.

sideB.text: An HTTP cycle has a request and a response.
The HTTP cycle consists of a request and a response.
An HTTP cycle includes a request and a response.
The HTTP cycle contains a request and a response.

cards.sideA: Un método HTTP POST contiene encabezados y un cuerpo.

sideB.text: An HTTP POST method has headers and a body.
The HTTP POST method contains headers and a body.
An HTTP POST method includes headers as well as a body.
An HTTP POST method contains headers and a body.

cards.note: ~An HTTP method POST has headers and a body~ uses an unnatural word order. In English, technical modifiers generally precede the noun they modify, making [HTTP POST method] the standard structure.

cards.sideA: La respuesta en un método HTTP también contiene un cuerpo.

sideB.text: The response in an HTTP method also has a body.
An HTTP response also contains a body.
The response of an HTTP method includes a body as well.

cards.sideA: El cuerpo y la carga útil son lo mismo en este contexto.

sideB.text: The body and the payload are the same in this context.
In this context, the body and the payload are identical.
Both body and payload refer to the same thing in this context.

cards.sideA: La respuesta HTTP tiene un código de estado y un cuerpo o carga útil.

sideB.text: An HTTP response has a status code and a body or payload.
The HTTP response contains a status code and a body or payload.
Every HTTP response includes a status code and a payload or body.

cards.sideA: A los encabezados enviados en una petición se les llama metadatos.

sideB.text: Headers sent in a request are called metadata.
The headers included in a request are referred to as metadata.
Request headers are known as metadata.

cards.note: ~In the headers sent in a request are called metadata~ is incorrect because the preposition 'In' leaves the verb 'are called' without a subject. Remove 'In' to make [The headers] the subject.

cards.sideA: La petición y la respuesta forman una transacción HTTP completa.

sideB.text: A request and a response form a complete HTTP transaction.
The request and response make up a full HTTP transaction.
An HTTP transaction is completed by a request and a response.
A request and a response are a complete HTTP transaction.

cards.sideA: Afecta a todas las provincias.

sideB.text: It affects all provinces.
It has an impact on all provinces.
All provinces are affected by this.
It affects all the provinces.

cards.sideA: Afecta a ambos sistemas operativos por igual.

sideB.text: It affects both operating systems equally.
Both operating systems are affected in the same way.
It has an equal impact on both OS.

cards.note:
**[191]**

~It affects both operating systems by equal~ is invalid because 'by equal' is a direct translation from Spanish. The correct adverb to express 'por igual' is [equally].

cards.sideA: No he mirado la hoja de trabajo.

sideB.text: I haven't looked at the spreadsheet.
I haven't checked the spreadsheet yet.
I haven't seen the spreadsheet.

cards.sideA: Estaré atento a la hoja de trabajo.

sideB.text: I'll keep an eye out for the spreadsheet.
I'll be on the lookout for the spreadsheet.
I'll stay tuned to the spreadsheet.

cards.sideA: Así que, desde mi punto de vista, sí funciona.

sideB.text: So, from my point of view, it works.
So, the way I see it, it does work.
So, as far as I'm concerned, it works.
The way I view it, it works.
As I view the situation, it works.
As I see it, it works.
As I see things, it works.
So, from my view, it works.

cards.sideA: Desde mi lado, no he podido replicar el problema.

sideB.text: From my side, I haven't been able to replicate the issue.
On my end, I couldn't reproduce the problem.
From where I stand, I haven't been able to duplicate the issue.

cards.note:
**[202]**



~From my point of view, I haven't been able to replicate the issue~ is unnatural here. [From my point of view] is used for sharing opinions, not technical environments. Use [On my end] or [From my side] when referring to your system or workstation.

cards.sideA: Desde nuestro lado, no hemos visto el problema aun.

sideB.text: From our side, we haven't seen the issue yet.
On our end, we haven't encountered the problem yet.
From where we stand, we haven't seen the issue so far.
On our side, we haven't seen the issue yet.
On our end, we haven't seen the issue yet.

cards.sideA: No he podido acceder.

sideB.text: I haven't been able to log in.
I couldn't access it.
I haven't been able to get in.

cards.sideA: Por favor, saca estas líneas del codigo.

sideB.text: Please remove these lines from the code.
Please strip out these lines.
Please drop these lines from the file.
Please delete these lines.

cards.sideA: Saca este codigo de la funcion.

sideB.text: Please remove this code from the function.
Please strip this code out of the function.
Please pull this code out of the function.
Please drop this code from the function.

cards.note:
**[200]**



~Please remove this code of the function~ is incorrect because you remove something [from] a source, not [of] it.

cards.sideA: Es lo mas probable.

sideB.text: It is most likely.
Most likely.
That is highly probable.
Highly likely.

cards.sideA: Tenemos que establecer el largo mínimo y maximo del numero.

sideB.text: We have to set the minimum and maximum length of the number.
We need to establish the min and max length for the number.
We must define the minimum and maximum length of the number.
We have to set the minimum and maximum length for the number.

cards.sideA: No queremos tener números decimales, solo enteros.

sideB.text: We don't want to have decimal numbers, only integers.
We don't want decimals, just whole numbers.
We only want integers, no decimal numbers.

cards.sideA: En cuanto a eso, puedo decirte que sí es correcto.

sideB.text: Regarding that, I can tell you that it is correct.
As for that, I can tell you it's correct.
Concerning that, I can confirm it is correct.
Regarding that, I can tell you it is correct.

cards.sideA: Sobre ese punto, confirmo que sí es correcto.

sideB.text: On that point, I confirm it is correct.
Regarding that point, I confirm it's correct.
About that point, I can confirm that it's correct.

cards.sideA: Hasta donde se, no se ha establecido aun.

sideB.text: As far as I know, it hasn't been established yet.
As far as I know, it hasn't been set.
To my knowledge, it hasn't been defined yet.
As far as I know, it hasn't been set yet.

cards.sideA: El Joker es el comodín.

sideB.text: The Joker is the wild card.
The Joker acts as the wild card.
The Joker serves as a wild card.

cards.sideA: Usa un comodín en la búsqueda.

sideB.text: Use a wildcard in the search.
Make use of a wildcard in the search.
Utilize a wildcard for the search.

cards.sideA: El asterisco funciona como comodín.

sideB.text: The asterisk functions as a wildcard.
The asterisk works as a wildcard.
The asterisk acts like a wildcard.

cards.sideA: El % funciona como comodín.

sideB.text: The % functions as a wildcard.
The % sign works as a wildcard.
The percent sign acts as a wildcard.
The % works as a wildcard.

cards.sideA: No tengo idea, déjame ver.

sideB.text: I have no idea, let me see.
I've got no clue, let me check.
I have no idea, let me take a look.
